title: Professors Sue Their Own University Over Protest Arrests
summary:
What would you do if your teacher called the police on you for something you didn't even do? That's kind of what three professors at Emory University in Atlanta are saying happened to them—except they're suing their own school!

In April 2024, some students set up tents on the university's main lawn to protest the war in Gaza. Three professors—Noelle McAfee, Emilio Del Valle-Escalante, and Caroline Fohlin—say they were just watching or trying to help when police and state troopers showed up and started arresting people.

McAfee says she yelled "Stop!" at an officer who was roughly arresting a student. She got charged with disorderly conduct. Del Valle-Escalante says he was trying to help an older woman when he was arrested. Fohlin says she protested when officers pinned a protester to the ground—and then she was thrown face-first to the ground, suffering a concussion (a brain injury) and a spine injury. She was charged with hitting an officer.

Here's the crazy part: none of them were convicted of anything. All charges were later dropped. But they still had to spend money on lawyers to defend themselves. Now they're suing the university to get that money back and to force the school to follow its own free-speech rules.

The university says the lawsuit is "without merit" and that they acted to keep everyone safe. But the professors say the school broke its own policies by calling police without trying other options first.

This isn't just about three professors. It's part of a bigger debate: Can you protest on a college campus without getting arrested? What do you think?
why_it_matters: This story is about the balance between safety and free speech, which affects students and teachers at schools everywhere.
